content: I'll start by reading my role, prompts, and the current state.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs (OpenAI/Gemini/OpenRouter) for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. **Requires `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` in env** — check via `env | grep -E "OPENAI|GEMINI|OPENROUTER"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | — | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Read the stage-specific prompt from `.archon/prompts/` (`prover-autoformalize.md` / `prover-prover.md` / `prover-polish.md`).
127	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
128	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
129	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
130	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
131	
132	### Subagents
133	
134	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
135	
136	### Review Agent
137	
138	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
139	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
140	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
141	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
142	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
143	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
144	
145	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
146	
147	These run automatically between phases:
148	
149	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
150	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
151	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
152

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. Log negative search results ("Searched 'projective module infinite rank' — nothing in Mathlib"). The plan agent merges these into `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md` next iter.
124	
125	**Read-only context**: read `task_pending.md` (prior attempts on your file) and `task_done.md` (when the problem resembles a completed one). Do not write to either.
126	
127	## End-of-session handoff
128

1	# Analogy: relative "proper-into-affine constant on fibres" as a morphism equality (Rigidity bridge 2)
2	
3	## Mode
4	api-alignment
5	
6	## Slug
7	rigidity-affineconst
8	
9	## Iteration
10	159
11	
12	## Question
13	Does Mathlib have an idiom for the **relative** statement "a proper morphism with
14	geometrically-connected fibres into an affine target is constant along fibres", expressed as a
15	**scheme-morphism equality**, usable to close the agreement equation of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`
16	(`AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:181`)? Concretely on `U = X × V`:
17	`U.ι ≫ f.left = U.ι ≫ (lift (toUnit (X⊗Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left`.
18	
19	## Project artifact(s)
20	- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:111-181` — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`; the
21	  agreement-equation `sorry` at L181 is "bridge 2".
22	- `AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean:243-274` — `rigidity_core` consumes it via
23	  `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`.
24	
25	## Decisions identified
26	
27	### Decision 1: the RELATIVE framing (Stein factorization / proper pushforward `f_* O_X = O_base`)
28	
29	- **Mathlib idiom**: **absent.** There is no `SteinFactorization`, no proper pushforward of the
30	  structure sheaf with a connectedness conclusion, and no proper base change for `H⁰`. Searches:
31	  `lean_leansearch "Stein factorization proper morphism pushforward structure sheaf"` returns only
32	  `SheafOfModules.pushforward` / `Scheme.Modules.pushforward` (the bare functor `f_*`, no
33	  `f_* O = O` theorem) and `TopCat.Sheaf.pushforward_sheaf_of_sheaf`. `loogle` for proper
34	  pushforward connectedness: nothing. The relative `Γ(X ×_S T) = Γ(X) ⊗ Γ(T)` (flat base change
35	  for `H⁰`) is also absent.
36	- **Project's path (if pursued)**: build `p₂,* O_{X×V} = O_V` and factor `f` through it.
37	- **Gap**: divergent-and-wrong as a *first* move — this framing requires `H⁰`-base-change /
38	  coherent-cohomology infrastructure Mathlib does not have. Multi-iter gap-fill.
39	- **Verdict**: NEEDS_MATHLIB_GAP_FILL (do **not** pursue this framing for bridge 2).
40	
41	### Decision 2: the GLOBAL-SECTIONS + per-slice field route ("route B", cohomology-free)
42	
43	- **Mathlib idiom present**:
44	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.ext_of_isAffine` (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry...`, via leanfinder): a map
45	    into an **affine** scheme is determined by its global-sections map `appTop`. THIS is the
46	    "morphism into affine determined by `Γ`" handle the directive hoped for.
47	  - `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`
48	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper`): `Γ(slice)` is a field, module-finite over the
49	    base field.
50	  - `IsAlgClosed` + finite/algebraic ⟹ trivial extension (`Mathlib.FieldTheory.IsAlgClosed.*`):
51	    at a **closed** point of a finite-type `k̄`-scheme the residue field is `k̄`, so
52	    `Γ(slice) = k̄` and the slice→affine map is a single `k̄`-point.
53	  - `JacobsonSpace`, `closedPoints`, `closure_closedPoints`, `LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace`
54	    (`Mathlib.Topology.JacobsonSpace`, `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.FiniteType`):
55	    closed points are **dense** in a finite-type `k̄`-scheme.
56	  - `ext_of_isDominant` / `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`
57	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Separated`): two maps out of a **reduced** scheme into a
58	    **separated** target agreeing on a **dominant** (= dense-range, `dominant_eq_topologically`)
59	    subobject are equal. (Already used by `rigidity_core`.)
60	  - `Scheme.fromSpecResidueField x` with `range = {x}` and `IsPreimmersion`
61	    (`Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.ResidueField`): the per-point probe.
62	- **The assembly (cohomology-free)**:
63	  1. For each **closed** point `y ∈ V`: `κ(y) = k̄`; the slice `X_y ≅ X` is proper integral over
64	     `k̄`, maps into the affine `U₀`; `isField_of_universallyClosed` + `finite_appTop` + alg-closed
65	     ⟹ `Γ(X_y) = k̄` ⟹ (via `ext_of_isAffine`) `f|X_y = const = (retract ≫ f)|X_y` **as
66	     morphisms into `U₀ ⊆ Z`** (the two ring maps `Γ(U₀) → k̄` have the same kernel = the point,
67	     and a `k̄`-algebra map to `k̄` is pinned by its kernel).
68	  2. The closed slices are jointly **dense** in `U` (`closure_closedPoints`, Jacobson). Feed a
69	     dominant probe (coproduct of the closed-point `fromSpecResidueField`s, or any dense-range
70	     witness) to `ext_of_isDominant` ⟹ `f|U = (retract ≫ f)|U`.
71	- **The one genuinely-missing connective**: a packaged "morphisms agreeing on a **dense set of
72	  closed points** are equal" hom-extensionality. Mathlib has only the **single-dominant-morphism**
73	  `ext_of_isDominant`; turning "agrees at every closed point" into one dominant probe needs an
74	  (infinite, `Set`-indexed) coproduct `∐_{x∈closedPoints U} Spec κ(x) → U` plus a `DenseRange`
75	  proof. This is buildable from present pieces, not a cohomology gap.
76	- **Gap**: divergent-but-tractable. ~2–3 iter assembly, **no cohomology**.
77	- **Verdict**: PROCEED (this is the route; idiom = `ext_of_isAffine` per-slice +
78	  `ext_of_isDominant` globalize).
79	
80	### Decision 3 (actionable signature point): `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` is missing from the chain
81	
82	- The closed-points route needs `κ(closed pt) = k̄`, i.e. `[IsAlgClosed kbar]`. But
83	  `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` / `rigidity_core` / `rigidity_lemma` (L111, L243, L324) carry only
84	  `[Field kbar]`. The downstream consumers (`morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const`,
85	  `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme`, L357/L406) **already** assume `[IsAlgClosed kbar]`, and the
86	  variable is literally named `kbar`.
87	- **Cost of NOT adding it**: forces the harder generic-fibre route, which needs "geometrically
88	  connected + geometrically reduced ⟹ `Γ = K`" (trivial-finite-extension upgrade over a
89	  *non*-alg-closed base via `Algebra.IsGeometricallyReduced` ⟹ separable + connected ⟹ trivial).
90	  That is a separate medium build Mathlib does not package.
91	- **Verdict**: ALIGN (recommendation): add `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` to the three chain lemmas. Low
92	  cost, consistent with naming and downstream, and unlocks the cohomology-free route.
93	
94	## Recommendation
95	
96	Do **not** build the relative Stein / `f_* O = O` statement for bridge 2 (Decision 1 — multi-iter
97	cohomology gap). Instead take **route B**: (i) add `[IsAlgClosed kbar]` to
98	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`/`rigidity_core`/`rigidity_lemma`; (ii) prove per-closed-slice constancy
99	into the affine `U₀` with `ext_of_isAffine` + `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
100	`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` + alg-closed-triviality; (iii) globalize to `U` with
101	`ext_of_isDominant` fed a dense-range probe built from `closure_closedPoints` /
102	`fromSpecResidueField`. The only thing Mathlib does not hand you ready-made is the
103	"dense-closed-points ⟹ hom-ext" globalization step (Decision 2's missing connective) — that is a
104	small bespoke build, **not** cohomology. Net: bridge 2 is a ~2–3 iter cohomology-free assembly, not
105	the 1-iter idiomatic call the directive hoped for, and not the hopeless cohomology gap it feared.

8	/-!
9	# Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-`0` curve into an abelian variety are constant
10	
11	This file scaffolds the project's **committed characteristic-free** genus-`0` route
12	(route (c)): the abelian-variety rigidity stack. It sits **upstream** of
13	`AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian` (it imports only `AlgebraicJacobian.Genus`), breaking the
14	`RigidityKbar → Rigidity → Jacobian` import cycle so that `genusZeroWitness` can consume the
15	genus-`0` rigidity keystone directly.
16	
17	The headline `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` is the char-free replacement (no
18	`[CharZero kbar]`) for `AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_over_kbar` of `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`
19	(which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and still carries `[CharZero]`).
20	
21	The minimal chain has four links:
22	
23	1. `rigidity_lemma` — the Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I): the cube-free, cohomology-free
24	   properness/closed-map entry point. **Proven** (iter-157–159) modulo bridge 2
25	   (`rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine`, the slice-constancy / affine-constancy equation),
26	   which is now **decomposed** (iter-161) into Step 2 `morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints` (PROVEN)
27	   and Step 1 `rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine` (per-closed-slice constancy). The
28	   `JacobsonSpace` discharge inside bridge 2 is closed; the deep *algebraic* content of Step 1 is
29	   PROVEN as `eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral`. The lone residual `sorry` of the chain is the
30	   *geometric* slice/section assembly of Step 1 (the residue probe is reduced to a `k̄`-point
31	   statement; what remains is the section over the slice + the integrality of the slice).
32	2. `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` — every morphism `ℙ¹ → A` into an abelian variety is
33	   constant (blocked downstream on the theorem of the cube).
34	3. `genusZero_curve_iso_P1` — a smooth proper geom-irred genus-`0` curve over `k̄` is
35	   isomorphic to `ℙ¹` (blocked on Riemann–Roch).
36	4. `rigidity_genus0_curve_to_grpScheme` — THE HEADLINE consumed by `genusZeroWitness`.
37	
38	See `blueprint/src/chapters/AbelianVarietyRigidity.tex` for the informal sketches and sources
39	(Mumford, *Abelian Varieties*, Ch. II §4, §6; Milne, *Abelian Varieties*, Prop. 3.10;
40	Hartshorne, *Algebraic Geometry*, Example IV.1.3.5).
41	
42	## Encoding notes (iter-157 scaffold)
43	
44	Mathlib `b80f227` packages no `ℙ¹` as a `Scheme`, so — following the established project idiom
45	(see `AlgebraicJacobian.RigidityKbar`) — the projective line is encoded by its abstract
46	characterisation: a smooth proper geometrically irreducible `Over (Spec (.of kbar))` of relative
47	dimension `1` with `genus = 0`. The signatures of declarations 1–3 are **provisional**
48	(`SCAFFOLD` comments mark them); the prover may refine the encoding when the bodies are filled.
49	Declaration 4 is pinned verbatim to `rigidity_over_kbar`'s signature minus `[CharZero kbar]`,
50	because it is the exact signature the consumer (`genusZeroWitness.key`) needs.
51	-/
52	
53	set_option autoImplicit false
54	
55	universe u
56	
57	open CategoryTheory Limits MonoidalCategory CartesianMonoidalCategory MonObj
58	
59	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
60	
61	variable {kbar : Type u} [Field kbar]
62	
63	/-- **Cartesian-monoidal identity (skeleton step of the Rigidity Lemma).** Post-composing the
64	second projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` with the slice section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` is the "collapse the
65	`X`-axis onto `x₀`" endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y`:
66	`snd ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) = lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`.
67	
68	Pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (no geometry): `comp_lift` distributes the `snd`, the
69	`𝟙 Y` component simplifies by `Category.comp_id`, and the `toUnit Y` component collapses by
70	uniqueness of maps into the terminal object. -/
71	theorem rigidity_snd_lift
72	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}
73	    (x₀ : 𝟙_ (Over (Spec (.of kbar))) ⟶ X) :
74	    snd X Y ≫ lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) =
75	      lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) := by
76	  ext1 <;> simp
77	
78	/-- **Bridge 1 of the Rigidity Lemma (closed-map step), PROVEN.** When `X` is complete (proper)
79	over `k̄`, the second monoidal projection `snd : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Y` has, on underlying schemes, a
80	*closed* base map. This is Mumford's "completeness of `X` makes `p₂` a closed map" (Abelian
81	Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43).
82	
83	Proof: the underlying scheme morphism `(snd X Y).left` is the pullback projection
84	`Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` (`Over.snd_left`), i.e. the base change of `X.hom` along
85	`Y.hom`. `IsProper X.hom ⟹ UniversallyClosed X.hom` (`IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
86	`UniversallyClosed` is stable under base change
87	(`universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback` on the canonical pullback square), so
88	`(snd X Y).left` is universally closed and hence its base map is closed
89	(`Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap`). Char-free; no theorem of the cube, no cohomology. -/
90	theorem snd_left_isClosedMap
91	    {X Y : Over (Spec (.of kbar))} [IsProper X.hom] :
92	    IsClosedMap (snd X Y).left.base := by
93	  haveI hp : UniversallyClosed X.hom := IsProper.toUniversallyClosed
94	  haveI : UniversallyClosed (snd X Y).left := by
95	    rw [Over.snd_left]
96	    exact universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange.of_isPullback
97	      (IsPullback.of_hasPullback X.hom Y.hom) hp
98	  exact Scheme.Hom.isClosedMap _

100	/-- **Dense-closed-points hom-extensionality (the bespoke globalisation connective, PROVEN).**
101	Two morphisms `g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z` out of a *reduced* scheme `W` whose closed points are *dense*
102	(`[JacobsonSpace W]` — e.g. when `W` is locally of finite type over a field) into a *separated*
103	scheme `Z` are equal as soon as they agree at every closed point `x ∈ closedPoints W` after the
104	canonical residue-field probe `W.fromSpecResidueField x : Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`.
105	
106	This is the one connective the iter-159 `mathlib-analogist` flagged that Mathlib does **not**
107	package directly: Mathlib supplies only the single-dominant-morphism `ext_of_isDominant`. Here we
108	assemble all the closed points into one dominant probe — the coproduct
109	`∐_{x ∈ closedPoints W} Spec κ(x) ⟶ W`, whose topological range is exactly the (dense) set of
110	closed points — and feed it to `ext_of_isDominant`. It is `Step 2` of bridge 2's route B
111	(cohomology-free) and is fully proven here, reusable independently of the rigidity context. -/
112	theorem morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints
113	    {W Z : Scheme.{u}} [IsReduced W] [JacobsonSpace W] [Z.IsSeparated]
114	    {g₁ g₂ : W ⟶ Z}
115	    (h : ∀ x ∈ closedPoints W,
116	      W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₁ = W.fromSpecResidueField x ≫ g₂) :
117	    g₁ = g₂ := by
118	  -- The dominant probe: the coproduct of the residue-field `Spec`s over the closed points.
119	  let F : closedPoints W → Scheme.{u} := fun x => Spec (W.residueField x.1)
120	  let probe : (∐ F) ⟶ W := Sigma.desc fun x => W.fromSpecResidueField x.1
121	  -- Its topological range contains every closed point, hence (Jacobson) is dense.
122	  haveI : IsDominant probe := by
123	    refine ⟨(dense_iff_closure_eq.mpr (closure_closedPoints (X := W))).mono ?_⟩
124	    intro x hx
125	    obtain ⟨pt⟩ : Nonempty (Spec (W.residueField x)) := inferInstance
126	    refine ⟨(Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩).base pt, ?_⟩
127	    have hcomp : Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩ ≫ probe = W.fromSpecResidueField x := Sigma.ι_desc _ _
128	    have e1 : probe.base ((Sigma.ι F ⟨x, hx⟩).base pt) = (W.fromSpecResidueField x).base pt := by
129	      rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_apply, hcomp]
130	    rw [e1]
131	    exact Set.eq_of_mem_singleton (Scheme.range_fromSpecResidueField x ▸ Set.mem_range_self pt)
132	  -- Componentwise the probe equalises `g₁` and `g₂`; dominance then forces `g₁ = g₂`.
133	  refine ext_of_isDominant probe (Sigma.hom_ext _ _ fun x => ?_)
134	  rw [← Category.assoc, ← Category.assoc, Sigma.ι_desc]
135	  exact h x.1 x.2

137	/-- **A proper integral `k̄`-scheme mapping into an affine is constant on `k̄`-points (the deep
138	algebraic content of Step 1, PROVEN).** Over an algebraically closed field `k̄`, let `W` be an
139	integral scheme that is universally closed and locally of finite type over `Spec k̄` (e.g. a proper
140	integral slice `X_y`), and let `g : W ⟶ V` be a morphism into an *affine* scheme `V`. Then `g`
141	takes the same value on any two `k̄`-points (sections `a`, `b` of the structure map `wk`):
142	`a ≫ wk = 𝟙` and `b ≫ wk = 𝟙` force `a ≫ g = b ≫ g`.
143	
144	This is the cohomology-free realisation of "a global regular function on a proper integral
145	`k̄`-variety is constant". The global sections `Γ(W, ⊤)` form a field
146	(`isField_of_universallyClosed`) that is module-finite over `k̄`
147	(`finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed`, hence the structure map is integral on `Γ`); algebraic
148	closedness collapses the finite extension (`IsAlgClosed.ringHom_bijective_of_isIntegral`), so the
149	structure map's global-sections map `wk.appTop` is an isomorphism. Both sections `a`, `b` are
150	left inverses of `wk.appTop` on `Γ`, hence have equal `appTop`; and a morphism into the affine `V`
151	is pinned by its `appTop` (`ext_of_isAffine`). No coherent cohomology, no relative Stein
152	factorisation. -/
153	theorem eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral
154	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
155	    {W : Scheme.{u}} [IsIntegral W] (wk : W ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar))
156	    [UniversallyClosed wk] [LocallyOfFiniteType wk]
157	    {V : Scheme.{u}} [IsAffine V] (g : W ⟶ V)
158	    (a b : Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar) ⟶ W)
159	    (ha : a ≫ wk = 𝟙 _) (hb : b ≫ wk = 𝟙 _) :
160	    a ≫ g = b ≫ g := by
161	  -- `Γ(W)` is a field (proper integral over `k̄`).
162	  letI : Field Γ(W, ⊤) := (isField_of_universallyClosed (CommRingCat.of kbar) wk).toField
163	  -- `F : k̄ ⟶ Γ(W)` (the structure ring map up to `ΓSpecIso`) is integral, hence — `k̄` alg-closed,
164	  -- `Γ(W)` a domain — bijective, so an iso.
165	  set F : CommRingCat.of kbar ⟶ Γ(W, ⊤) :=
166	    (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).inv ≫ wk.appTop with hF
167	  have hint : F.hom.IsIntegral := by
168	    apply RingHom.isIntegral_respectsIso.2 (e := (Scheme.ΓSpecIso _).symm.commRingCatIsoToRingEquiv)
169	    exact isIntegral_appTop_of_universallyClosed wk
170	  haveI : IsIso F := (ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective F).mpr
171	    (IsAlgClosed.ringHom_bijective_of_isIntegral F.hom hint)
172	  -- Hence `wk.appTop = ΓSpecIso.hom ≫ F` is an iso.
173	  haveI : IsIso wk.appTop := by
174	    have heq : wk.appTop = (Scheme.ΓSpecIso (CommRingCat.of kbar)).hom ≫ F := by
175	      rw [hF]; simp
176	    rw [heq]; infer_instance
177	  -- Both sections invert `wk.appTop` on global sections, so they have equal `appTop`.
178	  have haa : wk.appTop ≫ a.appTop = 𝟙 _ := by rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, ha]; simp
179	  have hbb : wk.appTop ≫ b.appTop = 𝟙 _ := by rw [← Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, hb]; simp
180	  have hab : a.appTop = b.appTop := by rw [← cancel_epi wk.appTop, haa, hbb]
181	  -- A map into the affine `V` is pinned by `appTop`.
182	  apply ext_of_isAffine
183	  rw [Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, Scheme.Hom.comp_appTop, hab]

271	
272	  `U.ι ≫ f.left = U.ι ≫ (retract ≫ f).left`.

274	This is the cohomology-free **route B** of the iter-159 `mathlib-analogist` consult
275	(`analogies/rigidity-affineconst.md`); it is the genuinely-deep residual of the Rigidity-Lemma

280	The intended proof (no coherent cohomology):
281	1. *Per closed slice.* For each closed point `y ∈ Vset`, `κ(y) = k̄` (`[IsAlgClosed kbar]`, finite
282	   type). Saturation puts the whole fibre `X_y` inside `U`, so `f` maps the proper integral slice
283	   `X_y ≅ X` into the affine `U₀`. By `isField_of_universallyClosed` +
284	   `finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` + alg-closedness, `Γ(X_y) = k̄`, so the slice maps to a
285	   single `k̄`-point of `U₀`
286	   (`ext_of_isAffine`); that point is `f(x₀, y)`, since `(x₀, y) ∈ X_y`. Hence `f` and `retract ≫ f`
287	   agree at every closed point of `U`.

290	   into one dominant probe (the coproduct `∐_{x∈closedPoints U} Spec κ(x) ⟶ U`, dense range) and

304	    (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens)
305	    (Vset : Set Y.left)
306	    (_hUV : (U : Set (X ⊗ Y).left) = (snd X Y).left.base ⁻¹' Vset)
307	    (U₀ : Z.left.Opens) (_hU₀ : IsAffineOpen U₀)
308	    (_hfU : ∀ u ∈ (U : Set (X ⊗ Y).left), f.left.base u ∈ U₀) :
309	    (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫ f.left =
310	      (U.ι : (U : (X ⊗ Y).left.Opens).toScheme ⟶ (X ⊗ Y).left) ≫
311	        (lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y) ≫ f).left := by
312	  -- Target separatedness (absolute), from `IsSeparated Z.hom` and the affine base `Spec k̄`:
313	  -- `terminal.from Z.left = Z.hom ≫ terminal.from (Spec k̄)`, a composite of separated maps.

315	    rw [Scheme.isSeparated_iff]
316	    have heq : terminal.from Z.left = Z.hom ≫ terminal.from (Spec (CommRingCat.of kbar)) :=

339	    rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine f x₀ U Vset _hUV U₀ _hU₀ _hfU x hx

370	theorem rigidity_eqOn_dense_open
371	    [IsAlgClosed kbar]
372	    {X Y Z : Over (Spec (.of kbar))}

479	/-- **Geometric core of the Rigidity Lemma (PROVEN, modulo `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`).** With
480	`X` complete (proper) and `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` a `k̄`-point, the morphism `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` is invariant
481	under the "collapse-the-`X`-axis-onto-`x₀`" endomorphism
482	`retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`:
483	
484	  `f = retract ≫ f`,
485	
486	i.e. `f(x, y) = f(x₀, y)` for all `(x, y)` — `f` depends only on the `Y`-coordinate. This is
487	the entirety of the *geometric* content of Mumford's Rigidity Lemma (Form I); the rest of
488	`rigidity_lemma` is the cartesian-monoidal algebra discharged by `rigidity_snd_lift`.
489	
490	This core is now **proven** by the project's scheme-level rigidity (replicated inline from
491	Mathlib's `ext_of_isDominant_of_isSeparated'`, since the wrapper `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen`
492	lives in the downstream `AlgebraicJacobian.Rigidity`): two maps out of the geometrically
493	irreducible reduced `X ⊗ Y` into the separated `Z` that agree on a non-empty open agree
494	everywhere. The single deferred input is the existence of that non-empty open together with the
495	agreement on it — `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` — which is the actual geometry below.
496	
497	This core carries the **collapse hypothesis** `_hf : f(X × {y₀}) = {z₀}` (encoded
498	`lift (𝟙 X) (toUnit X ≫ y₀) ≫ f = toUnit X ≫ z₀`), passed straight through to
499	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open`: it is exactly what makes Mumford's open `V := Y ∖ G` non-empty
500	(`y₀ ∉ G`). Without it the core statement is **false** (`f := fst` is not collapse-invariant).
501	
502	## Mumford's proof of `rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` (Abelian Varieties, Ch. II §4, p. 43)
503	
504	Set `g(y) = f(x₀, y)` (so `retract ≫ f` is exactly `(x, y) ↦ g(y)`). The open `V` is produced as
505	follows: let `U` be an affine open neighbourhood of a chosen point in `Z`, `F = Z ∖ U`, and
506	`G = (snd X Y) '' (f ⁻¹ F)`. Then for each `y ∈ V := Y ∖ G`, the slice `f(X × {y}) ⊆ U`, and `f`
507	agrees with `retract ≫ f` on the non-empty open `X × V`.
508	
509	This rests on **two char-free Mathlib bridges** (the cube-free heart); bridge 1 is now **built**,
510	bridge 2 (`rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine`) is now **decomposed and assembled** — Step 2
511	proven (`morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints`), Step 1's deep algebra proven
512	(`eq_comp_of_isAffine_of_properIntegral`); the lone residual `sorry` is Step 1's geometric
513	slice/section assembly (`rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`):
514	
515	  1. **Properness ⇒ the projection is a closed map — BUILT** (`snd_left_isClosedMap`, iter-158).
516	     `IsProper X.hom` is universally closed (`AlgebraicGeometry.IsProper.toUniversallyClosed`), and
517	     `snd X Y` is the base change of `X.hom` along `Y.hom`, hence universally closed, hence a closed
518	     map. The glue identifying the monoidal `snd X Y` in `Over (Spec k̄)` with the scheme-theoretic
519	     pullback projection `Limits.pullback.snd X.hom Y.hom` is the exact rewrite `Over.snd_left`, and
520	     `IsClosedMap` is transported across the canonical pullback square via
521	     `universallyClosed_isStableUnderBaseChange`. So `G = snd '' (closed)` is closed, `V` open.
522	
523	  2. **A proper connected variety mapping to an affine has image a single point.** For `y ∈ V`,
524	     the proper connected slice `X × {y}` maps under `f` into the affine `U`; a global regular
525	     function on a proper integral `k̄`-scheme is constant. Mathlib *has* the key fact:
526	     `AlgebraicGeometry.isField_of_universallyClosed` — for `X` integral and `f : X ⟶ Spec K`
527	     universally closed (`K` a field), `Γ(X, ⊤)` is a field. Combined with `Γ(affine U) → Γ(X×{y})`
528	     being a `k̄`-algebra map into a field that is finite over `k̄`
529	     (`AlgebraicGeometry.finite_appTop_of_universallyClosed` under `LocallyOfFiniteType`) and
530	     `k̄` algebraically closed, the map `X × {y} → U` factors through a single point. This is the
531	     "global-sections-constant" argument; assembling it into "image is one point" is the
532	     remaining work.
533	
534	These are exactly the "two Mathlib bridges to find/build" flagged in `PROGRESS.md`. Both bridges
535	are char-free (no theorem of the cube, no cohomology beyond `H⁰`). As of iter-159, bridge 1 is
536	built (`snd_left_isClosedMap`) and the non-emptiness fibre fact (`hfib`) is closed, so
537	`rigidity_eqOn_dense_open` is `sorry`-free in its own body; bridge 2 (slice-constancy) is now
538	decomposed and assembled (Steps 1–2), its lone residual `sorry` being Step 1's geometric
539	slice/section assembly (`rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine`). The
540	categorical reduction (`rigidity_lemma`, `rigidity_snd_lift`) and the scheme-level gluing
541	(`rigidity_core`) are closed. -/

556	  -- The gluing step is scheme-level rigidity: two maps out of a geometrically irreducible reduced

577	/-- **Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I).** Let `X` be a complete (proper) variety and `Y`, `Z`
578	any varieties over `k̄`. If `f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapses the slice `X × {y₀}` to a single point

589	## iter-157 signature correction (the scaffold was false as stated)
590	
591	The iter-157 scaffold carried only `[IsProper X.hom]`. **That statement is false**: take `X` =
592	two disjoint reduced `k̄`-points (proper but disconnected), `Y` = two points, `Z = X`, and
593	`f : X ⊗ Y ⟶ Z` collapsing the `y₀`-fibre to one point while separating the other fibre. Then
594	`_hf` holds yet `f` does not factor through `snd`. Mumford's hypothesis is that `X` is a complete
595	**variety** (irreducible) and `Y`, `Z` are **varieties**; the formal statement therefore needs
596	`X ⊗ Y` geometrically irreducible and reduced (so the dense-open rigidity glue applies) and `Z`
597	separated (so agreement on a dense open propagates). These three instances are added; they are
598	exactly what `Scheme.Over.ext_of_eqOnOpen` (the gluing step) consumes, and what Milne's Rigidity
599	Theorem 1.1 lists ("`V` complete, `V × W` geometrically irreducible, `Z` separated").
600	
601	## iter-157 prover progress
602	
603	The categorical *skeleton* is fully discharged here; only the single geometric core
604	remains. Concretely, Mumford "chooses any point `x₀ ∈ X`" and sets `g(y) = f(x₀, y)`. We
605	make this explicit by adding a `k̄`-point `x₀ : 𝟙_ ⟶ X` to the hypotheses (Mumford's
606	"complete variety `X`" is nonempty, so over `k̄` such a point exists; the downstream
607	consumer `morphism_P1_to_grpScheme_const` supplies it from `ℙ¹`). The witness is then
608	
609	  `g := lift (toUnit Y ≫ x₀) (𝟙 Y) ≫ f`   (the section `y ↦ (x₀, y)` post-composed with `f`).
610	
611	The goal `f = snd X Y ≫ g` rewrites — by pure cartesian-monoidal algebra (`comp_lift`,
612	`toUnit` uniqueness, `Category.comp_id`) — to
613	
614	  `f = retract ≫ f`,   where   `retract := lift (toUnit (X ⊗ Y) ≫ x₀) (snd X Y)`
615	
616	is the endomorphism `(x, y) ↦ (x₀, y)` of `X ⊗ Y` that collapses the `X`-axis onto `x₀`.
617	This reduction (lemma `rigidity_snd_lift`, then one `rw`) is closed below. The
618	remaining `f = retract ≫ f` is the genuine geometric heart (`rigidity_core`): `f` depends
619	only on the `Y`-coordinate. Its proof is Mumford's properness/closed-map/affine-constant
620	argument — see `rigidity_core` for the full decomposition into the two char-free bridges.

1	\chapter{Abelian-variety rigidity: morphisms from a genus-$0$ curve into an abelian variety are constant}
2	\label{chap:AbelianVarietyRigidity}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean
4	
5	% Local macro: diagrammatic (Lean `≫`) composition. Defined locally because this
6	% chapter is the writer's only editable file; flagged in the writer report for promotion
7	% to macros/common.tex if desired. \providecommand is a no-op if it is later added globally.
8	\providecommand{\fatsemi}{\mathbin{\,;\,}}
9	
10	% STRATEGY NOTE (iter-157). This chapter is the dedicated home of the project's COMMITTED
11	% genus-0 route (route (c)): the characteristic-free abelian-variety rigidity stack. It is
12	% 1:1 with the new upstream Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/AbelianVarietyRigidity.lean, which
13	% imports AlgebraicJacobian.Genus and is imported by AlgebraicJacobian.Jacobian (breaking the
14	% RigidityKbar -> Rigidity -> Jacobian import cycle so that genusZeroWitness can consume the
15	% keystone directly). The headline thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV is the char-free
16	% replacement (no [CharZero]) for the over-k-bar declaration rigidity_over_kbar of
17	% \cref{chap:RigidityKbar} (which remains in tree as the fallback route (a) artifact and
18	% still carries [CharZero]). The df=0 / H^0(P^1,Omega)=0 / Serre-duality framing is the
19	% fallback (a), NOT the route developed here.
20	
21	The genus-$0$ arm of the Jacobian-witness construction has a \emph{trivial} witness object
22	($J = \Spec k$); its only remaining mathematical content is a rigidity \emph{statement}:
23	every pointed morphism from a smooth proper geometrically irreducible curve of genus $0$
24	into an abelian variety is the constant morphism at the identity. We prove this
25	\textbf{characteristic-free} --- the protected goal is over an arbitrary field $k$, so the
26	cheaper differential argument (via $H^0(\mathbb P^1, \Omega) = H^0(\mathbb P^1, \mathcal
27	O(-2)) = 0$, hence $df = 0$, hence $f$ constant) is unavailable: it breaks on a
28	characteristic-$p$ Frobenius-descent wall, where a nonconstant $f$ can have $df = 0$. The
29	rigidity route below sidesteps that wall.
30	
31	The minimal chain has four links. The \emph{Rigidity Lemma} (\cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
32	Mumford, Form~I) is an elementary properness/closed-map argument --- it uses neither the
33	theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is the entry point for formalisation. The
34	\emph{theorem of the cube} (\cref{thm:theorem_of_the_cube}) enters only as the deep,
35	characteristic-free input that an abelian variety carries no nonconstant image of $\mathbb
36	P^1$ (the single-curve base case of Milne's Proposition~3.10); it is recorded here as a
37	\textbf{deferred} prerequisite with no formal proof yet. Combining the two gives that every
38	morphism $\mathbb P^1 \to A$ is constant (\cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant}); composing
39	with the classification of genus-$0$ curves (\cref{prop:genusZero_curve_iso_P1},
40	$C \cong \mathbb P^1$ over $\bar k$) yields the headline
41	\cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV}. This chain invokes neither Serre duality nor the FGA
42	$\Pic$ representability engine, so the genus-$0$ arm is fully decoupled from Route~A.
43	
44	\medskip
45	
46	Throughout, $\bar k$ is an algebraically closed field of arbitrary characteristic. An
47	\emph{abelian variety} over $\bar k$ is a complete (proper) irreducible group variety; in the
48	project's Lean encoding $A$ is an object of $\mathrm{Over}\,(\Spec \bar k)$ carrying
49	$\mathtt{GrpObj}$, $\mathtt{IsProper}$, $\mathtt{Smooth}$, and $\mathtt{GeometricallyIrreducible}$.
50	
51	\section{The Rigidity Lemma}
52	
53	\begin{lemma}
54	
55	\leanok
56	[Rigidity Lemma (Mumford, Form I)]
57	  \label{thm:rigidity_lemma}
58	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_lemma}
59	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
60	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
61	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
62	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
63	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
64	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
65	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43.}
66	  Let $X$ be a complete variety, and let $Y$, $Z$ be any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
67	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism such that for some $y_0 \in Y$ the image
68	  $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0 \in Z$. Then there is a morphism
69	  $g \colon Y \to Z$ with $f = g \circ p_2$, where $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
70	  projection; that is, $f$ depends only on the $Y$-coordinate, and $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ for any
71	  fixed $x_0 \in X$.
72	\end{lemma}
73	
74	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
75	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
76	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
77	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
78	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
79	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
80	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
81	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
82	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
83	% assertion."
84	\begin{proof}
85	
86	\uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
87	% NOTE (iter-159 review): the iter-157 unsoundness REMAINS REPAIRED. The collapse hypothesis
88	% (_hf : f(X x {y0}) = {z0}) is threaded through all three helpers (rigidity_lemma ->
89	% rigidity_core -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open) and GENUINELY CONSUMED in the body of
90	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open (proves y0 \notin G, the load-bearing non-emptiness of V := Y - G).
91	% The counterexample f = fst is excluded (under f = fst, _hf forces X to collapse to a point).
92	% rigidity_eqOn_dense_open is sorry-free in its OWN body (hfib, the pullback-fibre fact over the
93	% k-bar-point y0, is closed). rigidity_snd_lift and snd_left_isClosedMap are axiom-clean (no sorryAx).
94	% NOTE (iter-160): the \uses edges run FORWARD along the true Lean dependency order
95	% (rigidity_lemma -> rigidity_eqOn_dense_open -> rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine), so the
96	% not-proven status of the residual sorry propagates up and de-launders BOTH thm:rigidity_lemma and
97	% lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open. No backward edge from the leaf lemmas back up the chain.
98	% NOTE (iter-161): bridge 2 (rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine) was DECOMPOSED into two named
99	% top-level sub-lemmas along the route-B (cohomology-free) split:
100	%   - Step 2, morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints: "two morphisms agreeing at every closed point of a
101	%     reduced Jacobson source into a separated target are equal" -- PROVEN axiom-clean (the
102	%     dense-closed-points coproduct probe + ext_of_isDominant; the connective Mathlib lacks).
103	%   - Step 1, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine: Mumford's per-closed-slice constancy
104	%     -- the genuinely-deep residual, still `sorry`. THIS is now the chain's lone deep residual
105	%     (bridge 2's body is real assembly: Step 2 proven, the JacobsonSpace U instance now derivable).
106	% SIGNATURE CHANGE (iter-161, plan-authorized): Step 2's globalisation needs the closed points of
107	% the saturated open U to be DENSE, i.e. U Jacobson, which over Spec k-bar follows from (X tensor Y)
108	% being LOCALLY OF FINITE TYPE. So [LocallyOfFiniteType (X tensor Y).hom] is now carried on FIVE
109	% chain lemmas (rigidity_lemma, rigidity_core, rigidity_eqOn_dense_open,
110	% rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine, rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine), in
111	% addition to [IsAlgClosed kbar]. Free downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type
112	% over k-bar. The earlier "[IsAlgClosed] is the only added instance" claim is hereby retired.
113	
114	  Fix any point $x_0 \in X$ and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$ (the
115	  composite of $y \mapsto (x_0, y)$ with $f$). Since $X \times Y$ is a variety (in
116	  particular reduced and irreducible, so separated and with no embedded structure), to prove
117	  the equality of morphisms $f = g \circ p_2$ it suffices to prove they agree on a non-empty
118	  open subset of $X \times Y$.
119	
120	  Let $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U$ (a closed set),
121	  and set $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection
122	  $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a \emph{closed} map (this is the defining property of
123	  completeness/properness); hence $G$, the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$, is closed in
124	  $Y$. The hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U$ gives
125	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, so $y_0 \notin G$. Therefore
126	  $V := Y - G$ is a non-empty open subset of $Y$.
127	
128	  For each $y \in V$ we have $X \times \{y\} \cap f^{-1}(F) = \varnothing$, i.e.
129	  $f(X \times \{y\}) \subseteq U$. Thus the complete (proper) variety $X \times \{y\}$ is
130	  mapped by $f$ into the \emph{affine} variety $U$. A proper connected variety mapping to an
131	  affine variety has image a single point (a global regular function on a complete connected
132	  variety is constant). Hence $f(X \times \{y\})$ is one point, necessarily
133	  $f(x_0, y) = g(y)$. So for all $x \in X$ and $y \in V$,
134	  \[ f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g(y) = (g \circ p_2)(x, y). \]
135	  The two morphisms $f$ and $g \circ p_2$ thus agree on the non-empty open
136	  $X \times V$, which is dense in the irreducible $X \times Y$; as $Z$ is separated they
137	  agree everywhere. \qedhere
138	\end{proof}
139	
140	\begin{remark}
141	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_cube_free}
142	  The proof of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} uses \emph{only} (i) that completeness of $X$ makes
143	  $p_2$ a closed map, and (ii) that a proper connected variety has no nonconstant map to an
144	  affine variety. It uses neither the theorem of the cube nor any cohomology, and is valid in
145	  arbitrary characteristic. This is the designated formalisation entry point: it is the
146	  lowest-prerequisite link of the chain. (Mumford gives this as the geometric heart of the
147	  ``second proof'' of commutativity of an abelian variety; Milne packages the same statement
148	  as the Rigidity Theorem~1.1, requiring $V$ complete and $V \times W$ geometrically
149	  irreducible.)
150	\end{remark}
151	
152	\begin{remark}
153	  \label{rmk:rigidity_lemma_decomposition}
154	  The Lean formalisation of \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma} splits into three helper steps, mirroring
155	  the proof above: \texttt{rigidity\_snd\_lift} (the cartesian-monoidal algebra identifying the
156	  collapsed map $(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$ followed by $f$ with $g \circ p_2$);
157	  \texttt{rigidity\_core} (the scheme-level gluing: two maps out of the geometrically irreducible
158	  reduced $X \times Y$ into the separated $Z$ that agree on a non-empty open subset agree
159	  everywhere, by separatedness); and \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open}
160	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}, the genuine geometry: the construction of the non-empty
161	  open $X \times V$ and the slice-constancy of $f$ on it). The collapse hypothesis
162	  $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$ must be threaded through \emph{all three} helpers, since it is
163	  what makes $V$ non-empty; a decomposition that drops it from the lower helpers is unsound
164	  (see \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}).
165	
166	  \emph{Status (iter-161).} Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) is \textbf{built} as the axiom-clean
167	  helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap}. The fibre-over-$y_0$ fact is \textbf{closed} (via the
168	  coarse \texttt{PullbackCarrier} layer), so \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open} is
169	  \texttt{sorry}-free in its own body. Bridge~2 (slice-constancy on the saturated open $U$,
170	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}) has now been \textbf{decomposed} along its
171	  cohomology-free route-(c) split into two named sub-lemmas: the globalisation
172	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2, ``agree at every closed point of a reduced
173	  Jacobson source $\Rightarrow$ equal'') is \textbf{proven axiom-clean}, and the per-closed-slice
174	  constancy \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine} (Step~1, Mumford's slice
175	  argument) is the chain's \emph{single genuinely-deep residual} \texttt{sorry}. The body of
176	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} is therefore real assembly (Step~2 over Step~1).
177	
178	  This route requires the base field to be algebraically closed \emph{and} the source to be of finite
179	  type: Step~2 needs the closed points of $U$ to be dense (the Jacobson-space property), which over
180	  the algebraically closed $\bar k$ holds because $X \times Y$ is locally of finite type.
181	  Accordingly the formalization carries \textbf{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
182	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ across the chain lemmas
183	  (\texttt{rigidity\_lemma}, \texttt{rigidity\_core}, \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_dense\_open},
184	  \texttt{rigidity\_eqOn\_saturated\_open\_to\_affine},
185	  \texttt{rigidity\_eqAt\_closedPoint\_of\_proper\_into\_affine}); the earlier claim that
186	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ is the \emph{only} added instance is retired. Both cost nothing
187	  downstream: curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over the algebraically closed $\bar k$,
188	  and the consumers already assume it. The relative Stein-factorisation / proper-pushforward
189	  $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ framing is a genuine Mathlib cohomology gap and is \emph{deliberately
190	  avoided}.
191	\end{remark}

193	\begin{lemma}
194	
195	\leanok
196	[Dense-open agreement: the geometric heart of the Rigidity Lemma]
197	  \label{lem:rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rigidity_eqOn_dense_open}
199	  % SOURCE: [Mumford, Abelian Varieties], Rigidity Lemma (Form I.), Ch.~II \S4, book p.~43 (read from references/mumford-abelian-varieties.pdf, PDF page 54)
200	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "RIGIDITY LEMMA. (Form I.) Let $X$ be a complete variety, $Y$ and $Z$
201	  % any varieties, and $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ a morphism such that for some
202	  % $y_0 \in Y$, $f(X \times \{y_0\})$ is a single point $z_0$ of $Z$. Then there is a
203	  % morphism $g \colon Y \to Z$ such that if $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is the
204	  % projection, $f = g \circ p_2$."
205	  \textit{Source: Mumford, Abelian Varieties, Ch.~II \S4, Rigidity Lemma (Form~I), p.~43 (the
206	  dense-open construction is the body of that proof).}
207	  Let $X$ be a complete variety and $Y$, $Z$ any varieties over $\bar k$. Let
208	  $f \colon X \times Y \to Z$ be a morphism, fix a point $x_0 \in X$, and suppose given
209	  $y_0 \in Y$, $z_0 \in Z$ with the \emph{collapse hypothesis}
210	  \[ f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \qquad
211	     \text{(in Lean: } \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathbf 1_X)\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi y_0) \fatsemi f
212	     = \mathtt{toUnit}\,X \fatsemi z_0\text{).} \]
213	  Then there is a non-empty open subset $U \subseteq X \times Y$ on which $f$ agrees, as a
214	  scheme morphism, with the collapsed map $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$, where
215	  $\mathtt{retract} := \mathtt{lift}\,(\mathtt{toUnit}\,(X \times Y) \fatsemi x_0)\,
216	  (p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y)$ is the ``$(x, y) \mapsto (x_0, y)$'' endomorphism. Concretely
217	  $U = X \times V$ with $V := Y - G$, $G = p_2(f^{-1}(Z - U_0))$ for an affine open
218	  neighbourhood $U_0$ of $z_0$, and on $U$ one has $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y)$.
219	
220	  \medskip
221	  \noindent\textbf{The collapse hypothesis is load-bearing.} The non-emptiness of $V$ is exactly
222	  the assertion $y_0 \notin G$, and this holds \emph{because} $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}
223	  \subseteq U_0$ keeps the slice over $y_0$ inside the affine $U_0$, away from $F = Z - U_0$. A
224	  version of this lemma \emph{without} the collapse hypothesis is false: for $X = Y = Z$ and
225	  $f = p_1$ the first projection, $f$ and $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f \colon (x, y) \mapsto x_0$
226	  agree on no non-empty open (the agreement locus is contained in $\{x = x_0\}$, which has empty
227	  interior in the irreducible $X \times Y$). Therefore the formal target \emph{must} carry the
228	  collapse hypothesis as an explicit antecedent; dropping it (as an earlier decomposition
229	  silently did) makes the deferred goal unsatisfiable.
230	
231	  \medskip
232	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization note: algebraically closed base, and locally-of-finite-type
233	  source.} The prose already takes $\bar k$ algebraically closed, but the formal statement must
234	  \emph{encode} two properties. First, the cohomology-free proof of the slice-constancy bridge
235	  (below) needs each per-closed-point residue field $\kappa(y)$ to be $\bar k$ itself, so that a
236	  proper integral slice has global sections $\Gamma = \bar k$ and maps to a single $\bar k$-point of
237	  the affine $U_0$; this requires $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$. Second, the globalisation of the
238	  per-slice agreement (Step~2 of the bridge, \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints}) needs the
239	  closed points of the saturated open $U$ to be \emph{dense} --- i.e.\ $U$ to be a Jacobson space.
240	  Over the algebraically closed $\bar k$ this holds precisely because $X \times Y$ is \emph{locally of
241	  finite type} over $\bar k$ (\texttt{LocallyOfFiniteType.jacobsonSpace} transports the Jacobson
242	  property of $\Spec \bar k$, and \texttt{closure\_closedPoints} then gives density); this requires
243	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$. Accordingly the formalization carries
244	  \emph{both} $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ and
245	  $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$ on this lemma (in addition to
246	  $[\mathtt{Field}\ \bar k]$), and propagates the same two instances to \cref{thm:rigidity_lemma},
247	  its core helper \texttt{rigidity\_core}, and the bridge sub-lemmas
248	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} and
249	  \cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}. Both cost nothing downstream: the
250	  consumers \cref{prop:morphism_P1_to_AV_constant} and \cref{thm:rigidity_genus0_curve_to_AV} (Lean
251	  \texttt{rigidity\_genus0\_curve\_to\_grpScheme}) \emph{already} assume
252	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}\ \bar k]$ --- the base-field variable is literally named $\mathtt{kbar}$ ---
253	  and curves and abelian varieties are of finite type over $\bar k$, so the
254	  locally-of-finite-type instance is automatic there.
255	\end{lemma}
256	
257	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "PROOF. Choose any point $x_0 \in X$, and define $g \colon Y \to Z$ by
258	% $g(y) = f(x_0, y)$. Since $X \times Y$ is a variety, to show that $f = g \circ p_2$, it is
259	% sufficient to show that these morphisms coincide on some open subset of $X \times Y$. Let
260	% $U$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, $F = Z - U$, and $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$;
261	% then $G$ is closed in $Y$ since $X$ is complete and hence $p_2$ is a closed map. Further
262	% $y_0 \notin G$ since $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\}$. Therefore $Y - G = V$ is a non-empty
263	% open subset of $Y$. For each $y \in V$, the complete variety $X \times \{y\}$ gets mapped by
264	% $f$ into the affine variety $U$, and hence to a single point of $U$. But this means that for
265	% any $x \in X$, $y \in V$, $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = g \circ p_2(x, y)$, and this proves our
266	% assertion."
267	\begin{proof}
268	  \uses{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine}
269	  Let $U_0$ be an affine open neighbourhood of $z_0$ in $Z$, put $F = Z - U_0$, and set
270	  $G = p_2(f^{-1}(F))$. Because $X$ is complete, the projection $p_2 \colon X \times Y \to Y$ is a
271	  \emph{closed} map, so $G$ (the image of the closed set $f^{-1}(F)$) is closed in $Y$. The
272	  collapse hypothesis $f(X \times \{y_0\}) = \{z_0\} \subseteq U_0$ gives
273	  $f^{-1}(F) \cap (X \times \{y_0\}) = \varnothing$, hence $y_0 \notin G$; this is the only place
274	  the collapse hypothesis is used, and it is essential, for $V := Y - G$ is non-empty precisely
275	  because $y_0 \in V$. For each $y \in V$ the proper variety $X \times \{y\}$ is mapped by $f$
276	  into the affine $U_0$, hence to a single point, necessarily $f(x_0, y)$. So for all $x \in X$,
277	  $y \in V$, we have $f(x, y) = f(x_0, y) = (\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f)(x, y)$, i.e.\ $f$ and
278	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on the non-empty open $U := X \times V$. (In the Lean
279	  encoding the two slice-constancy bridges are the closed-map step from properness and the
280	  ``proper-into-affine has single-point image'' step; see the docstring of
281	  \texttt{rigidity\_core}.)
282	
283	  \medskip
284	  \noindent\textbf{Formalization notes (the concrete char-free Mathlib route).} The textbook
285	  proof above is realised in Lean by three pieces. They cite Mathlib lemma names as the chosen
286	  route; none uses cohomology, and the relative Stein-factorisation framing is deliberately
287	  \emph{avoided} (see below).
288	
289	  \emph{Bridge~1 (the closed-map step) --- BUILT.} ``$X$ complete $\Rightarrow p_2$ closed'' is
290	  the axiom-clean helper \texttt{snd\_left\_isClosedMap} (iter-158). It is obtained from
291	  \texttt{IsProper.toUniversallyClosed} on $X$, base-changed across the canonical pullback square
292	  to make $p_2$ universally closed, using that the monoidal projection \emph{is} the chosen
293	  pullback projection: \texttt{Over.snd\_left} rewrites $(\mathtt{snd}\,X\,Y).\mathtt{left}$ to
294	  $\mathtt{Limits.pullback.snd}\ X.\mathtt{hom}\ Y.\mathtt{hom}$ exactly. This step is already
295	  proven.
296	
297	  \emph{The fibre fact (non-emptiness of $V$) --- RESOLVED route, char-free.} That the slice
298	  $X \times \{y_0\}$ over the $\bar k$-rational point $y_0$ is exactly the image of the slice
299	  section $s \colon X \to X \times Y$ is formalised \emph{without} the residue-field /
300	  \texttt{Triplet} / tensor-product machinery (that fine \texttt{carrierEquiv} layer is the wrong
301	  granularity and is to be avoided). One pastes the identity square for the section against the
302	  canonical pullback square (\texttt{IsPullback.of\_right},
303	  \texttt{IsPullback.of\_horiz\_isIso}; the section identities $s \fatsemi p_1 = \mathbf 1$ and
304	  $s \fatsemi p_2 = X.\mathtt{hom} \fatsemi y_0$ come from \texttt{lift\_fst}/\texttt{lift\_snd}
305	  with \texttt{Over.w} on the unit) and reads off the fibre with the \emph{coarse} topological
306	  layer of \texttt{PullbackCarrier}, namely
307	  \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pullback.image\_preimage\_eq\_of\_isPullback}. This needs no
308	  $[\mathtt{IsAlgClosed}]$.
309	
310	  \emph{Bridge~2 (slice-constancy / the agreement equation) --- RESOLVED route, cohomology-FREE.}
311	  ``Proper connected slice into affine $\Rightarrow$ single point, glued to a morphism equality on
312	  $U$'' is realised by a \textbf{global-sections + per-closed-point} route, \emph{not} by any
313	  relative Stein-factorisation or proper-pushforward $f_*\mathcal O = \mathcal O$ statement: the
314	  latter is a genuine Mathlib gap (no Stein factorisation, no $H^0$ base change), and route~(c)
315	  exists precisely to avoid coherent cohomology, so it must \emph{not} be attempted. The concrete
316	  stack is: for each closed point $y \in V$ one has $\kappa(y) = \bar k$ (alg-closed, finite type);
317	  the slice $X_y$ is proper integral, so its global sections form a field
318	  (\texttt{isField\_of\_universallyClosed}) module-finite over $\bar k$
319	  (\texttt{finite\_appTop\_of\_universallyClosed}), and algebraic closedness kills the finite
320	  extension to give $\Gamma(X_y) = \bar k$. A morphism into the affine $U_0$ is pinned by its ring
321	  map on global sections (\texttt{ext\_of\_isAffine}), so $f$ and
322	  $\mathtt{retract} \fatsemi f$ agree on each closed slice. Closed points are dense in the
323	  locally-of-finite-type $U$ (\texttt{closure\_closedPoints} / the Jacobson-space property of
324	  locally-of-finite-type $\bar k$-schemes; in the formalization this density is supplied by the new
325	  hypothesis $[\mathtt{LocallyOfFiniteType}\ (X \otimes Y).\mathrm{hom}]$), so the per-slice
326	  agreement globalises to all of $U$ via the reduced-source / separated-target rigidity
327	  \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant\_of\_isSeparated'} (the same handle \texttt{rigidity\_core} already
328	  uses). The packaged ``morphisms agreeing on a dense set of closed points are equal''
329	  hom-extensionality --- the one connective Mathlib does not supply directly (it gives only the
330	  single-dominant-morphism \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}) --- has now been built as the axiom-clean
331	  \cref{lem:morphism_eq_of_eqAt_closedPoints} (Step~2): it assembles the closed points into one
332	  dominant coproduct probe and applies \texttt{ext\_of\_isDominant}. The whole argument is extracted
333	  into the dedicated obligation \cref{lem:rigidity_eqOn_saturated_open_to_affine} below, whose body
334	  wires that proven Step~2 over the per-slice Step~1
335	  (\cref{lem:rigidity_eqAt_closedPoint_of_proper_into_affine}); Step~1 (Mumford's per-closed-slice
336	  constancy) is now the chain's \emph{single} genuinely-deep residual \texttt{sorry}.
337	\end{proof}
